aichi.introTextEn: Home to the July Nagoya tournament—summer sumo at its hottest. Locals know their sumo and love hard-fought bouts in sweltering Nagoya.

akita.introTextEn: The Satake domain once promoted sumo; the prefecture produced ōzeki Kiyokuni. Many practice halls remain in the south, and snowy winters forge tenacious rikishi.

aomori.introTextEn: A northern powerhouse alongside Hokkaido—home to colorful stars like the first Wakahanada, Mainoumi, and Takamisakari. Sumo is woven into daily life; even young kids train in mawashi.

chiba.introTextEn: The prestigious Sadogatake stable is in Matsudo, tying the region tightly to sumo. Wrestlers raised on the teachings of yokozuna Kotoōshū connect deeply with local fans.

ehime.introTextEn: Nishiuwa’s historic Otoi Grand Sumo festival rivals pro events in energy. Sumo is a pillar of community life—among the strongest sumo cultures in Shikoku.

fukui.introTextEn: Part of the Hokuriku sumo belt with a long tradition. Many rikishi are compact technicians; local grit shows in their fighting spirit.

fukuoka.introTextEn: Hosts the November Kyushu basho. Kaiō—hero of the region—embodies powerful Kyushu sumo, with taiko-like intensity drawing strong men from across the island.

fukushima.introTextEn: Known as a “village of sumo,” energized by the Ōnami brothers. A family line from Wakabayama continues to inspire recovery and pride.

gifu.introTextEn: Rural areas keep dedication sumo and folk performance alive; sumo sits at the heart of community. Locals cheer plain-spoken, powerful hometown rikishi.

gunma.introTextEn: Birthplace of sekitori Tochikagayama. Dry northern winds build athletic rikishi; grass and dedication tournaments have deep roots here.

hiroshima.introTextEn: Home of yokozuna Akinoumi, who stopped Futabayama’s 69-bout streak. A city of peace that still honors rikishi who never back down on the dohyō.

hokkaido.introTextEn: Japan’s “sumo kingdom” with a record eight yokozuna. Taihō, Kitanoumi, Chiyonofuji, and others forged in snow and wide open land rewrote banzuke history.

hyogo.introTextEn: Led by ōzeki Takakeishō from Ashiya and fiery fighters like Myōgiryū. The spirit that overcame the Great Hanshin Earthquake shows in aggressive thrusting sumo.

ibaraki.introTextEn: Home of Japan-born yokozuna Kakuryū (now Nishonoseki oyakata). The prefecture still rallies behind him; the Great Buddha of Ushiku displays memorabilia.

ishikawa.introTextEn: Called the Hokuriku sumo kingdom—yokozuna Wajima, Endō, Terutsuyoshi, and more. Elite high-school sumo makes technique here among Japan’s best.

iwate.introTextEn: Lineage ties to Miyagino oyakata (ex-yokozuna Yoshibayama); Kinbōzan leads the modern era. Hachimantai tournaments carry honest, gritty “Nambu sumo” spirit.

kagawa.introTextEn: Source of thrusting specialists like Kagayaki. Like Sanuki udon—strong hips and never-say-die sumo are the trademark.

kagoshima.introTextEn: Kyushu’s sumo heartland—Kirishima and many sekitori. On the Amami islands, kids in mawashi are part of everyday life.

kanagawa.introTextEn: Birthplace of yokozuna Musashiyama; port-city Yokohama fused with sumo history. Shōnanoumi and others bring size and power from the coast.

kochi.introTextEn: Shikoku’s sumo kingdom—Toyonoshima, Tochinoshin (Meitoku Gijuku), and more. Fans love raw, forward-charging sumo like the famed Tosa fighting dog.

kumamoto.introTextEn: Former ōzeki Shōdai and others blend size and flexibility. The “Higo mokkotsu” stubborn streak shows in straight-ahead matches.

kyoto.introTextEn: Ancient capital where court ritual “Sumai no sechie” evokes sumo’s origins. Deep respect for tradition; young rikishi are rising again.

mie.introTextEn: Home of yokozuna Mienoumi. Dedication sumo at Ise Jingū ties the sport to sacred practice; Shimānoumi-style honest sumo is typical.

miyagi.introTextEn: Birthplace of legendary Tanikaze—the “strongest in Japan” in the Edo era. Sendai’s Tanikaze statue reflects enduring passion; local rikishi get roaring support.

miyazaki.introTextEn: Kinkōyū and others feel the prefecture’s warm backing. Bright yet resilient rikishi grow in a mild climate; the whole prefecture cheers like family.

nagano.introTextEn: Legendary Raiden Tameemon was born here. Today ōzeki Mitakeumi carries the torch—highland endurance shapes rikishi who never quit.

nagasaki.introTextEn: Home of yokozuna Sadanoyama’s relentless pushing style. A cosmopolitan port that still honors old-school, samurai-like discipline in sumo.

nara.introTextEn: Mythic birthplace where Nomi no Sukune and Taima no Kehaya duelled. Tokushōryū’s makushita championship reminded everyone of this sacred ground.

niigata.introTextEn: Home of yokozuna Haguroyama, rival to Futabayama’s streak. A rice country that produces sticky defense and horsepower on the dohyō.

oita.introTextEn: Birthplace of “god of sumo” yokozuna Futabayama—Usa’s Futaba no Sato honors him. Chiyotaikai and others continue the legacy.

okayama.introTextEn: Historic rikishi like Tsunenohana built steady western sumo. Young locals now climb the ranks with workmanlike, reliable sumo.

okinawa.introTextEn: Distinct Okinawan kakuriki culture differs from ōzumō; unique balance and legs feed individuality among Okinawa-born rikishi in pro sumo.

osaka.introTextEn: March tournament host—merchants’ city passion for winning. Ura and Gōeidō (now Takekuma oyakata) draw deafening hometown roars.

saga.introTextEn: Home of former ōzeki Saganohana—“swallow footwork” over the waves of Genkai. Rikishi here charge forward with simple, sturdy sumo.

saitama.introTextEn: A new powerhouse—Saitama Sakae High feeds Takakeishō, Kotonowaka, and more top names. Many stars honed basics in this belt of clubs.

shiga.introTextEn: Birthplace of yokozuna Onogawa Kisuke, rival to Tanikaze. Lake Biwa water faith ties to old tests of strength among locals.

shimane.introTextEn: Linked to Raiden and yokozuna Jinmaku. Dedication at Izumo Taisha keeps myth and sumo intertwined today.

shizuoka.introTextEn: Atamifuji and Suihō lead a local boom. Mild climate breeds open-hearted rikishi with Mount Fuji-scale presence.

tochigi.introTextEn: Yokozuna Tochigiyama set the tone—many use Tochi in their shikona. Locals favor plain, sturdy wrestling that backs sekitori.

tokushima.introTextEn: “Awa wonder kids” and dedicated coaches thrive here. Awa Odori rhythm meets skill over size in how fans appreciate rikishi.

tokyo.introTextEn: Sumo’s heart—Ryōgoku Kokugikan and many heya in shitamachi. Morning practice is part of the soundscape; also home to yokozuna Tochinohana lineage.

tottori.introTextEn: Tottori Johoku High is a powerhouse—Terunofuji, Ishihara, and Ōchiai trained here. A top pipeline feeding modern ōzumō.

toyama.introTextEn: Asanoyama’s rise fired up the prefecture; yokozuna Tachiyama came before. Fans love grand scale like the Tateyama peaks.

wakayama.introTextEn: Amateur monster Kushimaumi hailed from here. Commercial high schools are sumo powerhouses; warm climate meets fierce clinch sumo.

yamagata.introTextEn: Yokozuna Kashiwado built the “Hakuhō era” narrative. Bold attacking sumo is local pride; Kitanoō leads the next wave.

yamaguchi.introTextEn: Former ōzeki Kaijō—clean, upright sumo—and later association leader. Sincere, mentally strong rikishi match the prefecture’s character.

yamanashi.introTextEn: Ryūden’s never-say-die spirit comes from Kōfu basin winters. Passionate support groups back hometown rikishi through thick and thin.
